instruction: Implement a C++ thread pool that supports task submission with futures, priority scheduling, and graceful shutdown.
output:
<think>
A thread pool is a foundational concurrency primitive. The key components: a fixed set of worker threads, a thread-safe task queue, and a mechanism to return results (std::future via std::packaged_task).

Design decisions:
1. Priority queue: tasks have priority levels, higher priority tasks execute first
2. std::packaged_task + std::future for result retrieval
3. Condition variable for worker notification
4. Graceful shutdown: stop accepting new tasks, drain the queue, join threads
5. Work stealing could be added but is overkill for this — simple shared queue is fine for most use cases

The tricky part: std::packaged_task is move-only and not copyable, but std::function requires copyability. Solution: wrap in a type-erased move-only function wrapper, or use std::unique_ptr<void> with a custom deleter pattern.

Actually, the cleanest approach in modern C++: use std::function<void()> by wrapping the packaged_task in a shared_ptr. It's a small allocation but avoids the move-only headaches.

Alternatively, I can write my own move-only function wrapper. Let me do that for educational value — it's a common interview question and practically useful.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <condition_variable>
#include <functional>
#include <future>
#include <memory>
#include <mutex>
#include <queue>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>
#include <stdexcept>

// Move-only function wrapper (std::function requires copyable)
class MoveOnlyFunction {
    struct ImplBase {
        virtual ~ImplBase() = default;
        virtual void call() = 0;
    };

    template <typename F>
    struct Impl : ImplBase {
        F func;
        Impl(F&& f) : func(std::move(f)) {}
        void call() override { func(); }
    };

    std::unique_ptr<ImplBase> impl_;

public:
    MoveOnlyFunction() = default;

    template <typename F>
    MoveOnlyFunction(F&& f)
        : impl_(std::make_unique<Impl<std::decay_t<F>>>(std::forward<F>(f))) {}

    void operator()() {
        if (impl_) impl_->call();
    }

    explicit operator bool() const { return impl_ != nullptr; }
};

struct PriorityTask {
    int priority;  // Higher = more urgent
    int sequence;  // FIFO within same priority
    MoveOnlyFunction task;

    bool operator<(const PriorityTask& other) const {
        if (priority != other.priority)
            return priority < other.priority;  // min-heap, so < means lower priority
        return sequence > other.sequence;  // Earlier tasks first within same priority
    }
};

class ThreadPool {
public:
    explicit ThreadPool(size_t num_threads = std::thread::hardware_concurrency())
        : stop_(false), sequence_(0) {
        if (num_threads == 0) num_threads = 1;

        workers_.reserve(num_threads);
        for (size_t i = 0; i < num_threads; ++i) {
            workers_.emplace_back([this] { worker_loop(); });
        }
    }

    ~ThreadPool() {
        shutdown();
    }

    ThreadPool(const ThreadPool&) = delete;
    ThreadPool& operator=(const ThreadPool&) = delete;

    // Submit a task and get a future for the result
    template <typename F, typename... Args>
    auto submit(F&& f, Args&&... args) -> std::future<std::invoke_result_t<F, Args...>> {
        return submit_with_priority(0, std::forward<F>(f), std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }

    // Submit with explicit priority (higher = more urgent)
    template <typename F, typename... Args>
    auto submit_with_priority(int priority, F&& f, Args&&... args)
        -> std::future<std::invoke_result_t<F, Args...>> {

        using ReturnType = std::invoke_result_t<F, Args...>;

        auto task = std::make_shared<std::packaged_task<ReturnType()>>(
            std::bind(std::forward<F>(f), std::forward<Args>(args)...)
        );

        auto future = task->get_future();

        {
            std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
            if (stop_) {
                throw std::runtime_error("ThreadPool is shut down");
            }

            queue_.push(PriorityTask{
                .priority = priority,
                .sequence = sequence_++,
                .task = [task]() { (*task)(); },
            });
        }

        cv_.notify_one();
        return future;
    }

    void shutdown() {
        {
            std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
            if (stop_) return;
            stop_ = true;
        }
        cv_.notify_all();

        for (auto& worker : workers_) {
            if (worker.joinable()) {
                worker.join();
            }
        }
    }

    size_t pending() const {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
        return queue_.size();
    }

    size_t thread_count() const { return workers_.size(); }

private:
    void worker_loop() {
        while (true) {
            MoveOnlyFunction task;
            {
                std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
                cv_.wait(lock, [this] {
                    return stop_ || !queue_.empty();
                });

                if (stop_ && queue_.empty()) return;

                auto top = std::move(const_cast<PriorityTask&>(queue_.top()));
                task = std::move(top.task);
                queue_.pop();
            }
            task();
        }
    }

    std::vector<std::thread> workers_;
    mutable std::mutex mutex_;
    std::condition_variable cv_;
    std::priority_queue<PriorityTask> queue_;
    bool stop_;
    int sequence_;
};

// --- Usage ---
#include <iostream>
#include <numeric>

int main() {
    ThreadPool pool(4);

    // Submit tasks with different priorities
    std::vector<std::future<int>> futures;

    for (int i = 0; i < 20; ++i) {
        int priority = i % 3;  // 0, 1, 2
        futures.push_back(pool.submit_with_priority(priority, [i]() {
            std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(10));
            return i * i;
        }));
    }

    // Collect results
    int sum = 0;
    for (auto& f : futures) {
        sum += f.get();
    }
    std::cout << "Sum of squares: " << sum << std::endl;

    // Submit void tasks
    auto done = pool.submit([]() {
        std::cout << "Final task on thread "
                  << std::this_thread::get_id() << std::endl;
    });
    done.get();

    // pool.shutdown() called automatically in destructor
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a lock-free producer-consumer queue in C++ using std::atomic and compare-and-swap, with multiple producers and a single consumer.
output:
<think>
Lock-free data structures are hard to get right. The key primitive is CAS (compare_exchange_weak/strong). For an MPSC (multi-producer, single-consumer) queue, we can use a simpler design than a full MPMC queue.

Design options:
1. Michael-Scott queue: linked list with CAS on head/tail. Classic but has ABA problem.
2. LMAX Disruptor style: ring buffer with atomic sequence counters. Best performance but fixed size.
3. Intrusive linked list with reversed drain: producers push to a shared stack (LIFO via CAS), consumer reverses and processes (FIFO). This is the simplest correct MPSC.

I'll implement option 3 (Dmitry Vyukov's MPSC queue variant) because:
- Producers only need a single CAS on the head pointer
- Consumer just exchanges head with nullptr and reverses
- No ABA problem because we don't need to CAS on the same pointer twice
- Very cache-friendly for the consumer (sequential traversal)

Memory ordering:
- Producers: exchange with memory_order_acq_rel (need to see previous writes and publish new node)
- Consumer: exchange with memory_order_acquire (need to see all producer writes)

Memory management: nodes are allocated by producers, freed by consumer after processing. This avoids the hardest part of lock-free programming (safe reclamation).
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <cassert>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>

template <typename T>
class MPSCQueue {
    struct Node {
        T data;
        Node* next;
        Node(T&& val) : data(std::move(val)), next(nullptr) {}
    };

    std::atomic<Node*> head_{nullptr};  // Producers push here (LIFO stack)
    std::atomic<size_t> size_{0};

public:
    MPSCQueue() = default;
    ~MPSCQueue() {
        // Drain remaining items
        drain([](T&&) {});
    }

    MPSCQueue(const MPSCQueue&) = delete;
    MPSCQueue& operator=(const MPSCQueue&) = delete;

    // Thread-safe: multiple producers can call concurrently
    void push(T value) {
        auto* node = new Node(std::move(value));
        
        // CAS loop to prepend to the stack
        Node* old_head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        do {
            node->next = old_head;
        } while (!head_.compare_exchange_weak(
            old_head, node,
            std::memory_order_release,  // Publish node's data
            std::memory_order_relaxed));
        
        size_.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

    // Single consumer only: drain all items, process in FIFO order
    template <typename Func>
    size_t drain(Func&& process) {
        // Atomically steal the entire list
        Node* stolen = head_.exchange(nullptr, std::memory_order_acquire);
        if (!stolen) return 0;

        // Reverse the list to get FIFO order
        Node* reversed = nullptr;
        size_t count = 0;
        while (stolen) {
            Node* next = stolen->next;
            stolen->next = reversed;
            reversed = stolen;
            stolen = next;
            ++count;
        }

        // Process in FIFO order and free nodes
        while (reversed) {
            Node* next = reversed->next;
            process(std::move(reversed->data));
            delete reversed;
            reversed = next;
        }

        size_.fetch_sub(count, std::memory_order_relaxed);
        return count;
    }

    // Single consumer: try to pop one item
    bool try_pop(T& out) {
        Node* stolen = head_.exchange(nullptr, std::memory_order_acquire);
        if (!stolen) return false;

        // Reverse to get the oldest item
        Node* reversed = nullptr;
        while (stolen) {
            Node* next = stolen->next;
            stolen->next = reversed;
            reversed = stolen;
            stolen = next;
        }

        // Take the first (oldest) item
        out = std::move(reversed->data);
        Node* rest = reversed->next;
        delete reversed;

        // Put the rest back (still in LIFO order from the remaining reversed list)
        // Re-reverse back to stack order and push back
        if (rest) {
            Node* back_to_stack = nullptr;
            while (rest) {
                Node* next = rest->next;
                rest->next = back_to_stack;
                back_to_stack = rest;
                rest = next;
            }
            // Push the stack back
            Node* tail = back_to_stack;
            while (tail->next) tail = tail->next;
            
            Node* old_head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
            do {
                tail->next = old_head;
            } while (!head_.compare_exchange_weak(
                old_head, back_to_stack,
                std::memory_order_release,
                std::memory_order_relaxed));
        }

        size_.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
        return true;
    }

    size_t size_approx() const {
        return size_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

    bool empty() const {
        return head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed) == nullptr;
    }
};

// --- Benchmark ---

int main() {
    MPSCQueue<int> queue;
    constexpr int NUM_PRODUCERS = 4;
    constexpr int ITEMS_PER_PRODUCER = 100'000;
    
    std::atomic<bool> start{false};
    std::atomic<int> total_consumed{0};
    std::atomic<bool> producers_done{false};
    std::atomic<int> producers_finished{0};

    // Producer threads
    std::vector<std::thread> producers;
    for (int p = 0; p < NUM_PRODUCERS; ++p) {
        producers.emplace_back([&, p]() {
            while (!start.load()) { /* spin */ }
            for (int i = 0; i < ITEMS_PER_PRODUCER; ++i) {
                queue.push(p * ITEMS_PER_PRODUCER + i);
            }
            if (producers_finished.fetch_add(1) == NUM_PRODUCERS - 1) {
                producers_done.store(true, std::memory_order_release);
            }
        });
    }

    // Consumer thread
    std::thread consumer([&]() {
        while (!start.load()) { /* spin */ }
        long long sum = 0;
        int count = 0;

        while (!producers_done.load(std::memory_order_acquire) || !queue.empty()) {
            int batch = queue.drain([&](int&& val) {
                sum += val;
                ++count;
            });
            if (batch == 0) {
                std::this_thread::yield();  // Nothing to process
            }
        }

        // Final drain
        queue.drain([&](int&& val) {
            sum += val;
            ++count;
        });

        total_consumed.store(count);
        std::cout << "Consumer: processed " << count << " items, sum=" << sum << std::endl;
    });

    auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    start.store(true);  // Go!

    for (auto& t : producers) t.join();
    consumer.join();

    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(t1 - t0).count();

    int expected = NUM_PRODUCERS * ITEMS_PER_PRODUCER;
    int actual = total_consumed.load();
    std::cout << "Expected: " << expected << ", Got: " << actual << std::endl;
    std::cout << "Time: " << ms << "ms" << std::endl;
    std::cout << "Throughput: " << (actual * 1000LL / std::max(ms, 1LL)) << " items/sec" << std::endl;

    assert(actual == expected && "Item count mismatch!");
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement std::future and std::promise patterns in C++ for inter-thread communication, including chaining operations, shared futures, and combining multiple futures.
output:
<think>
std::future/std::promise is C++'s primitive for one-shot inter-thread communication. A promise is the write end, a future is the read end. Once set, the value can be retrieved exactly once (or shared via shared_future).

Key patterns to show:
1. Basic promise/future for passing a result from a worker thread
2. std::async for simplified future creation
3. shared_future for broadcasting a result to multiple waiters
4. Combining futures: wait for all, wait for first
5. Chaining: .then() doesn't exist in standard C++, but we can build it

The standard library is limited — no .then(), no when_all(), no when_any(). These exist in std::experimental::future or in libraries like folly. I'll implement them manually.

For when_all: spawn a thread that waits on all futures and collects results into a tuple/vector.
For when_any: spawn threads that each wait on one future and notify when done.
For then: spawn a thread that waits on the source future and applies the continuation.

Important details:
- future::get() can only be called once (moves the value out)
- shared_future::get() returns a const ref, can be called multiple times
- Promise must be kept alive until set — if destroyed without setting, the future gets a broken_promise exception
- std::async with launch::async guarantees a new thread
</think>

```cpp
#include <chrono>
#include <future>
#include <iostream>
#include <mutex>
#include <string>
#include <thread>
#include <tuple>
#include <vector>

// --- Building blocks not in std:: ---

// Chain a continuation onto a future (like .then())
template <typename T, typename F>
auto then(std::future<T> fut, F&& f) -> std::future<std::invoke_result_t<F, T>> {
    return std::async(std::launch::async,
        [fut = std::move(fut), f = std::forward<F>(f)]() mutable {
            return f(fut.get());
        });
}

// Specialization for void futures
template <typename F>
auto then(std::future<void> fut, F&& f) -> std::future<std::invoke_result_t<F>> {
    return std::async(std::launch::async,
        [fut = std::move(fut), f = std::forward<F>(f)]() mutable {
            fut.get();
            return f();
        });
}

// Wait for all futures, return vector of results
template <typename T>
std::future<std::vector<T>> when_all(std::vector<std::future<T>> futures) {
    return std::async(std::launch::async,
        [futs = std::move(futures)]() mutable {
            std::vector<T> results;
            results.reserve(futs.size());
            for (auto& f : futs) {
                results.push_back(f.get());
            }
            return results;
        });
}

// Wait for first completed future, return {index, value}
template <typename T>
struct FirstResult {
    size_t index;
    T value;
};

template <typename T>
std::future<FirstResult<T>> when_any(std::vector<std::future<T>> futures) {
    auto result = std::make_shared<std::promise<FirstResult<T>>>();
    auto done = std::make_shared<std::once_flag>();
    auto future = result->get_future();

    for (size_t i = 0; i < futures.size(); ++i) {
        std::thread([i, f = std::move(futures[i]), result, done]() mutable {
            try {
                T val = f.get();
                std::call_once(*done, [&]() {
                    result->set_value(FirstResult<T>{i, std::move(val)});
                });
            } catch (...) {
                // If this is the last one and all failed, we'd need special handling
                // For simplicity, first success wins
            }
        }).detach();
    }

    return future;
}

// Timeout wrapper
template <typename T>
std::optional<T> get_with_timeout(
    std::future<T>& fut,
    std::chrono::milliseconds timeout
) {
    if (fut.wait_for(timeout) == std::future_status::ready) {
        return fut.get();
    }
    return std::nullopt;
}

// --- Example services ---

std::string fetch_user(int user_id) {
    std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(100));
    return "User_" + std::to_string(user_id);
}

int fetch_order_count(const std::string& user_name) {
    std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(50));
    return 42;
}

double compute_score(int order_count) {
    std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(30));
    return order_count * 2.5;
}

int main() {
    // Pattern 1: Basic promise/future
    {
        std::promise<int> promise;
        auto future = promise.get_future();

        std::thread worker([&promise]() {
            int result = 42;  // expensive computation
            promise.set_value(result);
        });

        std::cout << "Result: " << future.get() << std::endl;
        worker.join();
    }

    // Pattern 2: Chaining (then)
    {
        auto result = then(
            then(
                std::async(std::launch::async, fetch_user, 123),
                fetch_order_count
            ),
            compute_score
        );

        std::cout << "Score: " << result.get() << std::endl;
    }

    // Pattern 3: Parallel fetch with when_all
    {
        std::vector<std::future<std::string>> futures;
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; ++i) {
            futures.push_back(std::async(std::launch::async, fetch_user, i));
        }

        auto all = when_all(std::move(futures));
        auto users = all.get();

        std::cout << "Fetched " << users.size() << " users: ";
        for (const auto& u : users) std::cout << u << " ";
        std::cout << std::endl;
    }

    // Pattern 4: shared_future for broadcast
    {
        auto shared = std::async(std::launch::async, []() {
            std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(100));
            return std::string("Config loaded");
        }).share();

        // Multiple threads can wait on the same result
        std::vector<std::thread> waiters;
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) {
            waiters.emplace_back([i, shared]() {
                const auto& config = shared.get();
                std::cout << "Thread " << i << " got: " << config << std::endl;
            });
        }
        for (auto& t : waiters) t.join();
    }

    // Pattern 5: Timeout
    {
        auto slow = std::async(std::launch::async, []() {
            std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::seconds(5));
            return 99;
        });

        auto result = get_with_timeout(slow, std::chrono::milliseconds(100));
        if (result) {
            std::cout << "Got: " << *result << std::endl;
        } else {
            std::cout << "Timed out" << std::endl;
        }
    }

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a C++ atomic-based spinlock with exponential backoff, a read-write spinlock, and a seqlock for read-heavy workloads.
output:
<think>
Atomic patterns in C++ are the building blocks of lock-free programming. Let me implement three increasingly sophisticated locking primitives:

1. Spinlock with backoff: basic mutual exclusion using atomic_flag or atomic<bool>. Exponential backoff reduces contention by having threads wait progressively longer instead of hammering the cache line.

2. Read-write spinlock: allows multiple concurrent readers OR one exclusive writer. Uses an atomic counter — positive means readers active, -1 means writer active. Readers increment, writer sets to -1.

3. Seqlock: optimistic reader pattern. Writer increments a sequence number before and after writing. Reader checks if sequence is even (no write in progress) and unchanged (no write during read). If not, retry. Zero overhead for readers on the happy path — no atomic writes, no cache invalidation.

Memory ordering is critical:
- Spinlock: acquire on lock, release on unlock
- RW lock: acquire/release semantics for the counter
- Seqlock: acquire fence on read, release fence on write. The sequence counter needs careful ordering.

I also need to handle the x86 PAUSE instruction (std::this_thread::yield or _mm_pause) to prevent pipeline stalls during spin waits.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <cassert>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>

// --- 1. Spinlock with exponential backoff ---

class SpinLock {
    std::atomic_flag flag_ = ATOMIC_FLAG_INIT;

public:
    void lock() {
        int backoff = 1;
        constexpr int MAX_BACKOFF = 1024;

        while (flag_.test_and_set(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
            // Exponential backoff to reduce cache-line bouncing
            for (int i = 0; i < backoff; ++i) {
                #if defined(__x86_64__) || defined(_M_X64)
                __builtin_ia32_pause();  // or _mm_pause()
                #else
                std::this_thread::yield();
                #endif
            }
            backoff = std::min(backoff * 2, MAX_BACKOFF);
        }
    }

    bool try_lock() {
        return !flag_.test_and_set(std::memory_order_acquire);
    }

    void unlock() {
        flag_.clear(std::memory_order_release);
    }
};

// --- 2. Read-Write Spinlock ---

class RWSpinLock {
    // Positive = number of readers, -1 = writer holds lock, 0 = free
    std::atomic<int32_t> state_{0};

public:
    void lock_shared() {
        while (true) {
            int32_t expected = state_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
            // Can acquire read lock if no writer (state >= 0)
            if (expected >= 0) {
                if (state_.compare_exchange_weak(
                        expected, expected + 1,
                        std::memory_order_acquire,
                        std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                    return;
                }
            } else {
                // Writer active, spin
                std::this_thread::yield();
            }
        }
    }

    void unlock_shared() {
        state_.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_release);
    }

    void lock() {
        while (true) {
            int32_t expected = 0;
            if (state_.compare_exchange_weak(
                    expected, -1,
                    std::memory_order_acquire,
                    std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                return;
            }
            std::this_thread::yield();
        }
    }

    void unlock() {
        state_.store(0, std::memory_order_release);
    }
};

// --- 3. Seqlock: optimistic reading ---

template <typename T>
class SeqLock {
    static_assert(std::is_trivially_copyable_v<T>,
                  "SeqLock requires trivially copyable types");

    std::atomic<uint32_t> seq_{0};
    T data_;

public:
    explicit SeqLock(const T& initial = T{}) : data_(initial) {}

    // Writer: exclusive access required (external synchronization among writers)
    void write(const T& value) {
        uint32_t s = seq_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        seq_.store(s + 1, std::memory_order_release);  // Odd = write in progress
        
        std::atomic_thread_fence(std::memory_order_release);
        data_ = value;
        std::atomic_thread_fence(std::memory_order_release);
        
        seq_.store(s + 2, std::memory_order_release);  // Even = write complete
    }

    // Reader: lock-free, retry on conflict
    T read() const {
        T result;
        uint32_t s1, s2;

        do {
            s1 = seq_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
            // If odd, a write is in progress — spin
            while (s1 & 1) {
                std::this_thread::yield();
                s1 = seq_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
            }

            std::atomic_thread_fence(std::memory_order_acquire);
            result = data_;  // Potentially torn read
            std::atomic_thread_fence(std::memory_order_acquire);

            s2 = seq_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        } while (s1 != s2);  // Retry if a write happened during our read

        return result;
    }
};

// --- Benchmark all three ---

struct Config {
    double rate;
    int max_connections;
    char name[32];
};

int main() {
    constexpr int NUM_THREADS = 8;
    constexpr int OPS_PER_THREAD = 100'000;

    // Benchmark 1: SpinLock
    {
        SpinLock lock;
        long counter = 0;
        auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

        std::vector<std::thread> threads;
        for (int i = 0; i < NUM_THREADS; ++i) {
            threads.emplace_back([&]() {
                for (int j = 0; j < OPS_PER_THREAD; ++j) {
                    lock.lock();
                    ++counter;
                    lock.unlock();
                }
            });
        }
        for (auto& t : threads) t.join();

        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(
            std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - t0).count();
        assert(counter == NUM_THREADS * OPS_PER_THREAD);
        std::cout << "SpinLock: " << ms << "ms" << std::endl;
    }

    // Benchmark 2: RWSpinLock (90% reads, 10% writes)
    {
        RWSpinLock rwlock;
        int shared_data = 0;
        std::atomic<long> read_sum{0};
        auto t0 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

        std::vector<std::thread> threads;
        for (int i = 0; i < NUM_THREADS; ++i) {
            threads.emplace_back([&, i]() {
                for (int j = 0; j < OPS_PER_THREAD; ++j) {
                    if (j % 10 == 0 && i == 0) {  // Only thread 0 writes
                        rwlock.lock();
                        shared_data = j;
                        rwlock.unlock();
                    } else {
                        rwlock.lock_shared();
                        read_sum.fetch_add(shared_data, std::memory_order_relaxed);
                        rwlock.unlock_shared();
                    }
                }
            });
        }
        for (auto& t : threads) t.join();

        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(
            std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - t0).count();
        std::cout << "RWSpinLock: " << ms << "ms" << std::endl;
    }

    // Benchmark 3: SeqLock
    {
        SeqLock<Config> config(Config{1.0, 100, "default"});
        std::atomic<bool> stop{false};
        std::atomic<long> reads{0};

        // Single writer
        std::thread writer([&]() {
            for (int i = 0; i < 10'000; ++i) {
                Config c;
                c.rate = static_cast<double>(i);
                c.max_connections = i;
                snprintf(c.name, sizeof(c.name), "cfg_%d", i);
                config.write(c);
                std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::microseconds(10));
            }
            stop.store(true);
        });

        // Multiple readers
        std::vector<std::thread> readers;
        for (int i = 0; i < NUM_THREADS; ++i) {
            readers.emplace_back([&]() {
                while (!stop.load(std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                    Config c = config.read();
                    (void)c;  // Use the value
                    reads.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
                }
            });
        }

        writer.join();
        for (auto& t : readers) t.join();

        std::cout << "SeqLock: " << reads.load() << " reads during 10k writes" << std::endl;
    }

    return 0;
}
```
